title: Sources of Genetic Variation
subtitle: Why siblings differ: reshuffling versus genuinely new alleles
subject: Biology
syllabusDotPoints: model the formation of new combinations of genotypes produced during meiosis, including crossing over, independent assortment and random fertilisation
explain how mutation introduces new alleles into a population
inquiryQuestion: How can the genetic similarities and differences within and between species be compared?
lessonIntent: Students can identify and explain the four sources of genetic variation and distinguish those that create new allele combinations from the one that creates new alleles.
examSkill: Classify a described source of variation as crossing over, independent assortment, random fertilisation or mutation, using precise new-combination versus new-allele wording.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Biology. Today, the sources of genetic variation. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Sources of Genetic Variation (Year 12 Biology, Module 5, Lesson 13)

scenes.heading: Why aren't siblings identical?
scenes.body: In the Galápagos finches, most heritable variation came not from new mutations, but from reshuffling alleles that already existed.
scenes.callout: Three mechanisms reshuffle, one creates. Which matters most?
scenes.caption: Grant and Grant measured beak-depth heritability at 0.79; most variation came from reshuffling existing alleles.

voiceover.text: From 1977 to 2012, Peter and Rosemary Grant measured the beaks of ground finches on a tiny Galápagos island, year after year. They found that seventy-nine percent of the variation in beak depth between birds was genetic. But here's the surprising part. Most of that variation didn't come from new mutations at all. It came from reshuffling alleles that the population already had. So think about your own family. Why are siblings clearly related, yet never identical, unless they're identical twins? The answer is that sexual reproduction has three ways to reshuffle existing alleles into new combinations, plus one way to create genuinely new alleles. Today we'll work through all four, and pin down the exact wording that separates them, because that wording is where marks are won and lost.

scenes.heading: Two flavours of variation
scenes.body: Variation comes from two different kinds of change: reshuffling existing alleles, and changing the DNA sequence itself.

bullets.text: Crossing over, independent assortment and random fertilisation reshuffle existing alleles.
These three make new combinations, not new alleles.
Mutation is the only source that creates genuinely new alleles.

scenes.secondary: This split is the spine of the whole lesson. Three mechanisms remix what's already there; one mechanism writes something new into the DNA.
scenes.callout: Reshuffle existing alleles, or write a new one. Two different things.
scenes.caption: Three mechanisms create new allele combinations; only mutation creates new alleles.

voiceover.text: So let's frame the whole lesson with one clean split. Genetic variation comes from two fundamentally different kinds of change. The first kind is reshuffling, taking alleles that already exist in the population and dealing them out in new combinations. Three mechanisms do this, crossing over, independent assortment, and random fertilisation. None of them invents a new allele, they just remix the existing ones. The second kind is changing the DNA sequence itself, and only one source does that, mutation. Mutation is the only way a genuinely new allele appears in the first place. Keep this split in your head for the rest of the lesson. Three mechanisms remix what's already there, and one mechanism writes something brand new into the DNA. Everything else is detail hanging off that frame.

scenes.heading: Two reshuffles inside meiosis
scenes.body: Meiosis itself contains two reshuffling mechanisms: crossing over and independent assortment.

bullets.text: Crossing over: homologous chromosomes swap segments, making recombinant chromosomes.
Independent assortment: homologous pairs line up randomly, mixing chromosomes.
Both make the gametes from one person genetically different from each other.

scenes.secondary: Crossing over reshuffles alleles along a chromosome; independent assortment reshuffles whole chromosomes between gametes. Different mechanisms, same effect: varied gametes.
scenes.callout: Crossing over swaps segments; assortment shuffles whole chromosomes.
scenes.caption: Crossing over and independent assortment both make genetically varied gametes during meiosis.

voiceover.text: Let's zoom into meiosis, because it actually contains two of our reshuffling mechanisms. The first is crossing over. During meiosis, homologous chromosomes line up and physically swap matching segments with each other. That produces recombinant chromosomes, carrying a new combination of alleles along their length. The second is independent assortment. When the homologous pairs line up to be separated, each pair orients completely at random, so different gametes end up with different mixes of whole chromosomes. Here's the nice way to keep them apart. Crossing over reshuffles alleles along a single chromosome, while independent assortment reshuffles entire chromosomes between gametes. They work differently, but the result is the same, the gametes produced by one single person are almost all genetically different from one another.

scenes.heading: The words you need

bullets.text: Crossing over: homologous chromosomes exchange segments during meiosis.
Independent assortment: random orientation of homologous pairs in meiosis.
Random fertilisation: the chance fusion of any one gamete with any other.
Mutation: a change in DNA sequence that can create a new allele.

scenes.callout: First three make combinations; mutation makes alleles.
scenes.caption: Crossing over, independent assortment, random fertilisation, mutation.

voiceover.text: Let's pin down the four words, because the exact phrasing matters here. Crossing over is when homologous chromosomes exchange segments during meiosis. Independent assortment is the random orientation of those homologous pairs as they separate in meiosis. Random fertilisation is the chance fusion of any one gamete with any other, so any sperm can meet any egg. And mutation is a change in the DNA sequence that can create a new allele. Notice the pattern one more time. The first three are all about combining alleles that already exist in new ways. Only the fourth, mutation, actually changes the sequence and produces a brand new allele. Same four words you'll need in every exam answer on variation.

scenes.heading: Fertilisation multiplies it again
scenes.body: Random fertilisation stacks even more variation on top of meiosis: any gamete can fuse with any other.

bullets.text: Each parent already makes hugely varied gametes through meiosis.
Any one gamete can then fuse with any one from the other parent.
That gives an enormous number of possible zygote genotypes.

scenes.secondary: This is why siblings are similar but not identical. They draw from the same allele pool, but meiotic shuffling plus random fertilisation makes each combination unique. Identical twins need a separate developmental event.
scenes.callout: Varied gametes, combined at random, equals unique offspring.
scenes.caption: Random fertilisation combines already-varied gametes, so each zygote genotype is essentially unique.

voiceover.text: Now add the third reshuffling mechanism, random fertilisation, and the numbers explode. Remember, each parent is already producing a huge range of genetically different gametes thanks to crossing over and independent assortment. Random fertilisation means any one of those gametes can fuse with any one of the gametes from the other parent. So you take an already enormous variety of sperm, an already enormous variety of eggs, and you combine them completely at chance. The number of possible zygote genotypes becomes staggering. And this is exactly why ordinary siblings turn out similar but not identical. They're drawing alleles from the same two parents, so there's real resemblance, but meiotic shuffling plus random fertilisation makes each combination essentially unique. Identical twins are the exception, and they need a separate developmental event, not ordinary fertilisation.

scenes.heading: Mutation writes something new
scenes.body: Mutation is the one source that changes the DNA sequence, so it is the only way a genuinely new allele appears.

bullets.text: A mutation changes the DNA sequence itself.
That can create an allele the population never had before.
Meiosis and fertilisation can only reshuffle alleles that already exist.

scenes.secondary: Precise wording for the exam: mutation makes new alleles; crossing over, independent assortment and fertilisation make new combinations. Don't call every source a mutation.
scenes.callout: New allele in the gene pool? That had to start as a mutation.
scenes.caption: Mutation changes DNA sequence to create new alleles; the other three only recombine existing ones.

voiceover.text: So where do new alleles actually come from in the first place? Only mutation. A mutation is a change in the DNA sequence itself, and that change can produce an allele the population has never had before. This is the crucial contrast. Meiosis and fertilisation are powerful, but they can only ever reshuffle alleles that already exist, they can't invent one. So if you ever see a genuinely new allele appear in a gene pool, it had to start life as a mutation. And here's the precise wording I want you to lock in for the exam. Mutation creates new alleles. Crossing over, independent assortment, and random fertilisation create new combinations of alleles. Get that distinction exactly right, and don't fall into calling every source of variation a mutation.

scenes.heading: Sort the source
scenes.question: Classify each as crossing over, independent assortment, random fertilisation or mutation: (a) homologous chromosomes swap segments; (b) a new allele appears from a base change; (c) any sperm may fertilise any egg.
scenes.coachNote: Ask the key question first: is a NEW allele made, or just a new combination?
steps: (a) Segments swapped between homologous chromosomes during meiosis → crossing over.
(b) A base change makes an allele that did not exist before → mutation (the only new-allele source).
(c) Any one gamete fusing with any other at random → random fertilisation.
Decision rule: new allele means mutation; new combination means one of the other three.
Answer: (a) crossing over, (b) mutation, (c) random fertilisation ✓
scenes.caption: (a) crossing over, (b) mutation, (c) random fertilisation — sorted by new allele vs new combination.

voiceover.text: Let's sort three examples, the way an exam will ask you to. Before each one, ask the key question, is a new allele being made, or just a new combination of existing alleles? Part a, homologous chromosomes swap segments with each other. That's segments being exchanged during meiosis, so that's crossing over, a new combination. Part b, a new allele appears because of a base change in the DNA. A genuinely new allele can only come from one place, so that's mutation. Part c, any sperm may fertilise any egg. That's gametes combining at random, so that's random fertilisation, again a new combination. So the decision rule is simple. If a brand new allele is created, it's mutation. If it's just existing alleles being mixed into a new arrangement, it's one of the other three. New allele, mutation; new combination, the rest.

scenes.heading: Two traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: saying crossing over creates new alleles. It does not. It reshuffles existing alleles into new combinations; only mutation creates new alleles.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: assuming all genetic variation is visible in the phenotype. Much of it is hidden, in heterozygous carriers of recessive alleles, in silent base changes and in non-coding regions. Phenotypic variation is only the part that gets expressed.
scenes.callout: Crossing over recombines, it doesn't create. Variation can be hidden.
scenes.caption: Crossing over recombines existing alleles, not new ones; much variation is cryptic, not visible.

voiceover.text: Two traps that quietly cost marks. Trap one, writing that crossing over creates new alleles. It absolutely does not. Crossing over reshuffles alleles that already exist into new combinations along a chromosome. The only source that creates genuinely new alleles is mutation, so keep those verbs separate, recombines versus creates. Trap two is more subtle. Students assume that all the genetic variation in a population must show up in the phenotype. It doesn't. A huge amount of variation is hidden, or cryptic. It's carried silently in heterozygous individuals who hold a recessive allele without showing it, in base changes that don't change the protein, and in non-coding regions of the DNA. So the phenotypic variation you can see is only the fraction that happens to be expressed. There's almost always more underneath.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: A population of beetles suddenly shows a brand new shell colour never seen before in that species. Which single source of variation must have been involved, and why?
answerSteps: A colour never seen before means a genuinely new allele has appeared.
Crossing over, independent assortment and fertilisation only recombine existing alleles.
None of those three can produce an allele the population never had.
Only mutation changes the DNA sequence to create a new allele.
Answer: mutation, because it is the only source of genuinely new alleles ✓
scenes.caption: A brand new trait needs a new allele, so mutation must be involved.

voiceover.text: Quick check. A population of beetles suddenly shows a brand new shell colour that has never been seen in that species before. Which single source of variation must have been involved, and why? Pause and reason it through. Okay, let's work it out. The key phrase is never seen before, which tells us a genuinely new allele has appeared in the population. Now run through our sources. Crossing over, independent assortment, and random fertilisation can only recombine alleles that already exist, so none of them can produce something the population never had. That rules out all three reshuffling mechanisms. The only source that actually changes the DNA sequence and creates a brand new allele is mutation. So mutation must have been involved, because it is the only way a genuinely new allele, and therefore a genuinely new trait, can first appear.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: There are four sources of genetic variation: crossing over, independent assortment, random fertilisation, mutation.
Crossing over and independent assortment reshuffle alleles during meiosis.
Random fertilisation combines varied gametes, so siblings are similar but not identical.
Mutation is the only source that creates genuinely new alleles.
Precise wording: mutation makes new alleles; the other three make new combinations.
scenes.finalPrompt: Classifying a source? Ask first: is a new allele made (mutation), or just a new combination (the other three)?
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: the four sources, the two meiotic shuffles, fertilisation, mutation, the wording.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, there are four sources of genetic variation, crossing over, independent assortment, random fertilisation, and mutation. Second, crossing over and independent assortment both reshuffle existing alleles during meiosis, one along the chromosome, one between chromosomes. Third, random fertilisation combines those already varied gametes, which is why ordinary siblings are similar but not identical. Fourth, mutation is the only source that creates a genuinely new allele, by changing the DNA sequence. And fifth, the wording that wins marks, mutation makes new alleles, while the other three make new combinations of alleles. So whenever you classify a source, ask first, is a new allele being made, which is mutation, or just a new combination, which is one of the other three. See you next lesson.
